sound_type: loud bubbly song over rushing water -- continuous varied bubbling stream of notes, high and low, trills and warbles, competing with stream noise, E=5
ecosystem: Fast stream -- clear, fast-flowing mountain streams with rocky bottoms, rapids, and cascades. Cascade and Olympic mountain streams. Year-round resident. Territory is a linear stretch of stream (300m-2km).
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. May move to lower elevations in winter when high-elevation streams freeze. Sings year-round including winter.
family: Cinclidae (FIRST ENTRY -- NEW FAMILY in the engine)

coordinates.description: Reference for mountain stream habitat in the central Cascades. American Dippers found in fast-flowing mountain streams throughout western North America.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST CINCLIDAE ENTRY -- NEW FAMILY in the engine. North America's ONLY truly aquatic songbird. Breaks the Picidae nonet (107-115). Fire-to-water transition: burn forest (115) to fast stream (116). Major taxonomic event. The dipper family has 5 species worldwide; the American Dipper is the sole North American representative.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Cinclidae count: 1 entry (American Dipper 116). NEW FAMILY.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cinclus from Greek kinklos -- a name used by Aristotle for a small, tail-wagging waterside bird. mexicanus from Latin -- 'of Mexico,' referring to where the type specimen was collected. The American Dipper: the American waterside bird.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cinclus -- the dipper genus. Five species worldwide. Only one in North America. The genus is the only truly aquatic passerine genus in the world.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes returns. After nine Piciformes (Picidae 107-115), the engine returns to Passeriformes with an aquatic songbird. Passeriformes doing something no other passerine does.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cinclidae is new. First entry at degree 116. Follows the Picidae nonet (107-115). A new era begins.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 2,000-8,000 Hz. Song frequency placed above dominant stream noise (which peaks below 2,000 Hz). The acoustic window above water noise.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 3,000-6,000 Hz. The loud, bubbly song concentrates energy in the mid-to-high frequency range where stream noise is lower.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Song: 2,000-8,000 Hz, continuous varied bubbling. Calls: sharp zeet contact note, 4,000-7,000 Hz.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Song: continuous, several minutes to 10+ minutes. A flowing stream of phrases delivered without pause. Among the longest continuous songs of any North American songbird.
song_metrics.note_structure: RIVER SONG: Continuous varied stream of notes -- high trills, low warbles, chattering phrases, musical whistles -- strung together in rapid unpredictable sequence. Compared to Winter Wren but louder, longer, more varied. Both males and females sing.
song_metrics.note_types: Song (primary, territorial/pair-bond), zeet contact call (sharp, high-pitched), alarm calls. The song is the diagnostic vocalization -- complex, bubbly, continuous.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual phrases strung together in unpredictable sequence. No fixed repertoire order. Each song performance is unique. The flowing, improvisational quality parallels the stream itself.
song_metrics.timbre: Bubbly. Musical. Liquid. The timbre is warm and varied -- shifting between bright trills and mellow warbles. The 'bubbly' quality is diagnostic. The song sounds like water translated into music.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- remarkably loud for a 60-gram bird. Must compete with rushing water. The volume is necessary for audibility in the noisiest natural environment a songbird encounters.
song_metrics.tempo: Rapid. Phrases delivered quickly, flowing into one another without pause. The continuous delivery is essential for signal persistence above variable water noise.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND PAIR-BOND. Sung from prominent rocks in mid-stream, facing upstream. Year-round, including winter. Both sexes sing. The song declares: this stretch of stream is occupied.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Moderate. Individual variation is high (each bird's song is unique). Regional dialects less documented than in some species. The song's complexity provides individual signatures.
song_metrics.key_property: LOUD BUBBLY SONG OVER RUSHING WATER -- the river singer. E=5. A 60-gram bird out-singing a mountain torrent. Frequency placement above stream noise (2,000+ Hz). High amplitude. Complex, continuous, varied. FIRST CINCLIDAE -- NEW FAMILY. North America's ONLY aquatic songbird. Breaks the Picidae nonet. Fire-to-water transition (115 burn forest to 116 fast stream). Major taxonomic event.

plumage.overall: Stocky, uniformly dark gray songbird. Short tail. Slightly brownish head in some light. White eyelid (nictitating membrane) visible when bird blinks. Compact, rounded body shape adapted for underwater movement.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 116: (1) UNIFORMLY DARK GRAY -- no wing bars, no eye ring, no streaking. Plain. (2) STOCKY, SHORT-TAILED -- compact body shape. (3) DIPPING BEHAVIOR -- constant rhythmic bobbing on rock perch. (4) STREAM HABITAT -- always on or in fast-flowing water. (5) WHITE EYELID FLASH -- visible when bird blinks (nictitating membrane).
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Juveniles have slightly paler, more brownish plumage with faint scaling on underparts.
plumage.flight_pattern: Low, fast, direct flight along stream corridors. Usually follows the stream course rather than flying overland. Flight is buzzy and rapid, close to the water surface.

physical_description.overall: Stocky songbird. Body length 16.5-19 cm. Wingspan 22-28 cm. Weight 46-83 g. Uniformly dark gray. Short tail. Strong legs and feet. Compact, streamlined body for underwater movement.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 116: stocky dark gray bird bobbing on a rock in fast-flowing stream. No markings. If it plunges into the water and walks along the bottom -- American Dipper. Nothing else does this.
physical_description.field_id: Small, dark gray, stocky bird bobbing on rocks in mountain streams. Plunges into fast-flowing water and walks underwater. Loud bubbly song above the roar of water. If a bird is walking underwater in a mountain stream -- it is an American Dipper. No other North American bird does this.

nodes.title: Cinclidae -- NEW FAMILY -- Major Taxonomic Event
nodes.description: First Cinclidae entry in the engine. An entirely new family. The only aquatic passerines in the world. Five species globally; one in North America. Degree 116 introduces a fundamentally new way of being a songbird.

nodes.title: The River Singer -- Out-Singing the River
nodes.description: Loud bubbly song competing with rushing water. Frequency placement above stream noise (2,000+ Hz). Remarkably loud for a 60-gram bird. Complex, continuous, varied. The acoustic challenge: sing over a mountain torrent. E=5.

nodes.title: Aquatic Adaptations -- Evolutionary Marvel
nodes.description: Nictitating membrane (underwater vision), dense plumage (~3,000 feathers), 10x preen gland (waterproofing), nasal flaps, strong legs/feet, elevated hemoglobin. No other songbird has this suite. Walks underwater in fast-flowing streams.

nodes.title: Fire-to-Water Transition (115-116)
nodes.description: Burn forest (Black-backed WP, 115) to fast stream (American Dipper, 116). Fire to water. The engine's most dramatic ecosystem transition. Picidae closes; Cinclidae begins. Dead trees to living water.

nodes.title: The Dipping Behavior -- Diagnostic Bobbing
nodes.description: Constant rhythmic up-and-down bobbing on rock perch. Nearly continuous when perched. Function debated. Diagnostic for identification: if a small gray bird is bobbing on a rock in a stream, it is an American Dipper.

cross_references.relationship: FIRE-TO-WATER TRANSITION. Burn forest (115, fire ghost, E=5) to fast stream (116, river singer, E=5). Most dramatic ecosystem transition. Fire to water. Charred snag fields to rushing mountain streams.
STREAM-ADJACENT. Chickadee forages in canopy above the stream; dipper forages in the stream below. Vertical habitat partitioning. Both year-round PNW residents.
FIRST PICIDAE vs FIRST CINCLIDAE. Flicker: first Picidae (degree 21). Dipper: first Cinclidae (degree 116). Both family firsts. Introducing woodpeckers vs. introducing aquatic songbirds.
MOUNTAIN HABITAT. Both montane. Chickadee in high-elevation conifer forest; Dipper in high-elevation mountain streams. Different niches, same elevation band.
ACOUSTIC ENVIRONMENT CONTRAST. Spotted Owl: silence of old-growth. Dipper: roar of rushing water. Quietest vs. loudest natural environments. Opposite acoustic challenges.
